article_title: Mitochondrial DNA Analysis of 2,456 Genomes Confirms Humans Reached Australia 60,000 Years Ago via Two Separate Maritime Routes

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)

content_preview.title: Mitochondrial DNA Analysis of 2,456 Genomes Confirms Humans Reached Australia 60,000 Years Ago via Two Separate Maritime Routes
content_preview.summary: An analysis of 2,456 mitochondrial genomes published in Science Advances identifies two distinct maritime routes humans used to reach Sahul roughly 60,000 years ago.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A large-scale analysis of mitochondrial DNA has provided the most detailed genetic evidence to date that modern humans reached the combined landmass of New Guinea and Australia -- known to researchers as Sahul -- approximately 60,000 years ago. The study, published in *Science Advances*, examined [2,456 mitochondrial genomes from Aboriginal Australians, New Guineans, and populations across Southeast Asia and the western Pacific](https://phys.org/news/2025-12-humans-australia-years-r...

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong archaeology/genetics article on human migration to Australia.
editor_notes.source_verification: ScienceDaily: VERIFIED — 2456 genomes, 60000 years, two routes, Huddersfield/Southampton, Science Advances. Phys.org and Live Science in source snapshots.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims verified: genome count, dating, dual-route hypothesis, institutional affiliations.
editor_notes.originality: No prior human migration or Australian settlement coverage.
